All clean. Here is the full analysis.

---

# Phase 1 analysis — gradient cost & Enter-key backlog lag

## Question 1: Do gradients have a real efficiency cost?

**Answer: yes, measurable, but far below "matters in practice" for prompt latency in Go terms (~microseconds). The one place gradients genuinely cost milliseconds is not Go at all — it's the PowerShell reader thread parsing the ~2x larger output (see Question 2, where this becomes the actual story).**

### The suspected benchmark degradation is real (confirmed)

`BenchmarkWriteGradientChained` (`src/terminal/gradient_bench_test.go:33`) is silently broken by our `ParentColors` reset. Confirmed empirically, not just by code-read: iteration 1 emits 383 bytes of per-cell gradient stamps; iteration 2 emits a 60-byte solid fallback. Mechanism: `String()` (`src/terminal/writer.go:609`) resets `ParentColors` to length 0; on the next iteration `resolveParentColor` (`src/color/keywords.go:31`) never enters its loop, `parentBackground` comes back unresolved, `GradientCells` (`src/color/gradient.go:128`) ends with <2 valid stops, returns nil, and `Write` collapses to the last stop as a solid. The old 1727ns/776B/28allocs number measured that degraded error path (the extra 12 allocs are `log.Errorf` varargs boxing), not chained-gradient rendering.

Side discovery while validating: the committed benchmark string contains two nerd-font glyphs (`\357\207\222`, `\356\253\267`) that don't survive copy-paste — a retyped "identical" benchmark measures 2 fewer cells and one fewer builder growth step (1048 vs 1753 B/op). Any corrected benchmark should reuse the literal from the file, not retype it.

### Trustworthy numbers (temp benchmarks, seeded `ParentColors` per iteration, all deleted after)

Per-`Write` of one 18-cell segment (glyph-free string, consistent across variants):

| variant | ns/op | B/op | allocs/op |
|---|---|---|---|
| solid | ~1,080 | 456 | 16 |
| gradient (steady state) | ~1,800 | 1,048 | 16 |
| chained gradient, corrected | ~1,750–1,850 | 1,048 | 16 |

So a gradient segment's `Write` is **~65–70% slower and ~2.3x the bytes of a solid segment — an absolute delta of ~0.7–1µs per segment**. A chained (`parentBackground`) gradient costs the same as a literal one once it actually resolves.

Two structural findings that frame this:

- **`gradientCellCache`** (`src/color/gradient.go:164`): interpolation results are memoized on resolved-stop RGB + cell count + channel + TrueColor. Cold interpolation (`BlendHcl` per cell) measured ~11.3µs/1,152B/26 allocs for 18 cells; a cache hit is ~240ns/144B/4 allocs. In the long-lived serve daemon the cache persists across cycles, so steady-state renders pay the cache-hit price. Cost scales with visible cell count (per-cell HCL blend + one alloc per cell via `ansiFromColorful`), stop count only affects the resolution prologue.
- **Zero-gradient configs pay nothing meaningful**: `Write` gates everything behind `IsGradient()` (a `strings.HasPrefix` check, `src/terminal/writer.go:399-445`) and takes the pre-gradient fast path `writeBody`. Confirmed by benchmark: solid B/op and allocs are identical to the pre-gradient-feature values.

Full-engine confirmation (temp `Engine.Primary()` benchmark, 2-segment config): solid ~17.8µs/9.8KB vs both-segments-gradient ~20.7µs/13.7KB — **+2–3µs and +40% bytes per render**. Even a 10-gradient theme adds ~10–15µs to a render whose floor is tens of milliseconds. In Go terms: noise.

**Caveat that turned out to matter**: the gradient prompt *output string* is ~2x larger (measured with Jan's real theme: 1,534 vs 729 bytes for the primary prompt). That's irrelevant to Go but expensive to the PowerShell byte-at-a-time reader — see below.

## Question 2: Enter-key backlog drain regression

### What's confirmed by measurement

Built and benchmarked 6 binaries (ba107a3f = daemon introduction, b95ad1a3, 29bd48bc, e98db08a, 8f4f7c89, HEAD) against a protocol driver that mimics the omp.ps1 waiter (send render, block for first matching record, repeat — exactly the drain pattern of queued Enters). Jan's real theme (`~/.posh.omp.jsonc`, `"streaming": 20`, 20 gradients), cwd = this repo, two passes, stable results:

| build | steady-state ms/cycle (gradient theme) | (solid-ized theme) |
|---|---|---|
| ba107a3f (daemon intro) | 26.6 | — |
| b95ad1a3 / 29bd48bc / e98db08a | 26.8–27.6 | — |
| **8f4f7c89 (gradients)** | **31.9–33.3** | 28.5–28.7 |
| HEAD (incl. ParentColors fix) | 32.3–32.8 | 28.8–29.4 |

End-to-end synchronous `prompt` function calls in a real pwsh with each build's own generated init script: **33.6ms (old) → 41.5ms (HEAD)** average.

So the bisect is clean: the only meaningful per-cycle regression since the daemon was built is **+5–6ms, arriving with `8f4f7c89` and only when the theme uses gradients**. Everything else (`971e150b` repaint, `ba1b9eb1` cmdline_url, `367ec833` right transient, template-data, PATH-rehash) measured flat. `Engine.Abort()` is not the problem: in the drain pattern the previous producer is parked in its `select` on `e.abort` (`src/prompt/streaming.go:124-127`) and exits immediately; measured cycle times confirm no abort stall.

### Where the +5ms actually comes from — and why it looks like seconds

The Go-side gradient cost is microseconds, so ~4ms of the delta had to be elsewhere. It's the **record pipeline byte volume**:

- Gradient theme primary record: **1,534 bytes** vs solid **729 bytes** (measured, Jan's theme, width 120). Transient: 328 bytes. Every streamed update record repeats the full primary size.
- The omp.ps1 reader runspace (`$script:StreamingReaderScript`, `src/shell/scripts/omp.ps1:452-474`) consumes daemon stdout **one `ReadByte()` at a time in interpreted PowerShell**. Measured throughput on this machine: **9.3µs/byte ≈ 9.3ms per KB**.
- So each cycle's records cost the reader thread ~14ms (gradient) vs ~7ms (solid) just to ingest the primary, plus the transient and any update records; the waiter can't see a record until the reader finishes it. My driver pays the same per-byte pattern, which is why it faithfully reproduces the +4–5ms.

### The qualitative flip ("used to stop instantly, now drains for seconds")

Confirmed by code-read as inherent architecture, unchanged since the daemon landed: each buffered Enter runs `prompt()` synchronously (omp.ps1:850-941 → `Get-PoshStreamingPrompt` :603-766), which blocks on a full round trip. No coalescing anywhere. So drain time = queued Enters x per-Enter cost — this was always true.

What changed is which side of the **key-repeat threshold** the system sits on. Windows key repeat is ~30 Hz — a ~33ms budget per Enter. When per-Enter cost is at/below the repeat interval (the ~27ms daemon + light script overhead of two weeks ago), the queue never grows while holding Enter, and releasing the key stops the prompt instantly. When per-Enter cost exceeds it (measured 42ms synchronous now, plus PSReadLine redraw, transient repaint via `InvokePrompt`, FTCS writes, and reader-thread contention in a real terminal — plausibly 60–100ms total), the queue grows for the entire hold and then drains at per-Enter cost: 2 seconds of held Enter → 30–60 queued → several seconds of drain. **A modest absolute regression crossing that threshold produces exactly the dramatic behavioral flip Jan describes.** The trigger was double-barreled: the gradient feature landed *and* Jan's theme adopted 20 gradients the same day.

Plausible-but-unverified amplifier: under spam, if a cycle's transient record hasn't been parsed by the reader when the next Enter lands, the transient path falls back to a full `oh-my-posh print transient` process spawn (~70–100ms) per Enter (omp.ps1:896-913). Narrow race in the measurements above, wider when the reader is behind; I did not reproduce it in isolation.

### Proposed fix scope (proposal only — awaiting your go)

Ranked by leverage, all independent:

1. **Replace the per-byte reader loop** (`src/shell/scripts/omp.ps1:452-474`) with buffered reads (`$stream.Read(byte[], …)` into a buffer, split on NUL). This is a pure client-side change, turns ~9.3µs/byte into ~amortized-zero, and shrinks every cycle by 10–40ms — almost certainly enough to get back under the key-repeat threshold even with gradients. Low risk, no protocol change.
2. Optional, additive: **skip the full cycle for empty-line Enters** — an Enter on an empty buffer re-renders a prompt whose context can't have changed; the script could reuse the cached prompt (needs care with `$script:Streaming.State` semantics, cwd/exit-code unchanged checks). Higher risk, bigger win under spam specifically.
3. Not worth pursuing: Go-side gradient optimization (already µs-level) or `Abort()` changes (not implicated).

## Flagged item: `terminal.String()` callers vs the `ParentColors` reset — real regression confirmed

`shouldFill` (`src/prompt/engine.go:166-202`) renders `block.Filler` *after* `renderBlockSegments` → `terminal.String()` (`src/prompt/segments.go:85`) has wiped the parent stack. **Confirmed reachable, with a repro**: a right-aligned prompt block with `"filler": "<parentBackground>-</>"` renders the filler in the block's segment background pre-fix (`ESC[38;2;0;0;255m` per dash) and in default foreground (`ESC[39m`) on HEAD. Repro config shape: any theme with a right-aligned block (or transient prompt, via `src/prompt/extra.go:98`) whose filler references `parentBackground`/`parentForeground` or a `linear-gradient(parentBackground, …)`.

Bounding the blast radius (verified): **zero stock themes use `filler` at all**, and zero stock themes use parent keywords in the first segment of a non-first block (the other shape whose behavior changed — pre-fix, block N's first segment inherited block N-1's tail colors through the never-reset stack; post-fix each block starts empty). So this affects only custom configs with those exact shapes. Note the old behavior was itself an artifact of the leak — in a daemon session the "parent" could be a *previous prompt's* stale colors — so the new per-block semantics are arguably the correct ones; the decision needed is whether filler should see the just-rendered block's tail (e.g. capture the tail colors before the reset, or reset in the engine after filler rendering instead of inside `String()`). One adjacent detail worth noting for any follow-up: with a non-nil empty parent stack, `Resolve` now returns the raw keyword string (`"parentBackground"`) instead of `Transparent` (`src/color/keywords.go:74-78` path) — harmless today because `ToAnsi` degrades it to no color, but it's an inconsistency vs the nil-parents path.

Also flagged: `BenchmarkWriteGradientChained` needs re-seeding of `ParentColors` per iteration (or an engine-level replacement) to measure what its name claims.

All temp benchmarks, worktrees, and test binaries were removed; working tree is clean.

This is just the analysis phase, so I'm holding off on any implementation until we get the go-ahead.

content.text:
**Gradient cost:** Go-side, negligible. ~1.8µs vs ~1.1µs per segment `Write`, +40% bytes on a full render (~20µs vs ~17µs total). Noise next to render floor. Old `BenchmarkWriteGradientChained` numbers were bogus — our `ParentColors[:0]` reset broke it after iteration 1 (parentBackground unresolved, silently collapsed to solid fallback). Fable re-seeded it correctly and confirmed: chained gradient costs same as literal once it actually resolves.

**Enter-lag: real regression, root cause found, not what we thought.** Bisected clean to `8f4f7c89` (gradient feature), only when theme uses gradients. Not `Abort()`, not our fix, not the other recent commits (repaint/cmdline_url/right-transient) — all measured flat.

Actual mechanism: gradient theme output is ~2x bytes (1534 vs 729B, your theme). `omp.ps1`'s reader (`src/shell/scripts/omp.ps1:452-474`) reads daemon stdout **one byte at a time in interpreted PowerShell** — 9.3µs/byte. Round-trip went 33.6ms → 41.5ms. Windows key-repeat is ~30Hz (~33ms budget). Old cost sat under that line — queue never grew while holding Enter, released instantly. New cost sits over it — queue grows the whole time you hold Enter, then drains at ~40ms/Enter after release. Small absolute regression, but crossing that threshold flips behavior from "instant" to "visibly draining for seconds." Matches what you're seeing exactly.

**Proposed fix (not done, waiting on your go):**
1. Replace `omp.ps1`'s per-byte reader loop with buffered reads — client-side only, no protocol change, should claw back the 10-40ms and put you back under the key-repeat line even with gradients. This is the real fix.
2. Optional: skip full render cycle for empty-line Enter (reuse cached prompt). Bigger win under spam, more risk, separate task.

**Also flagged, separate from above** — our `ParentColors` fix has a real edge-case regression: `block.Filler` (or transient) templates referencing `<parentBackground>`/`<parentForeground>` across the segment/filler boundary now resolve wrong (confirmed repro). Zero stock themes hit this (none use `filler` with parent keywords), so low blast radius, but it's a genuine behavior change from what we shipped. Needs a decision: capture tail colors before reset, or move reset point. Not fixing until you say which.

Want me to do the reader-loop fix now, and separately decide what to do about the filler edge case?

I found the streaming reader script and need to optimize it to buffer reads instead of processing one byte at a time. The approach is to read chunks of data (around 4096 bytes), scan through each chunk for NUL delimiters to split records, and carry over any incomplete bytes to the next read cycle. When the stream reaches EOF, I'll flush any remaining partial data as a final record.

The key is preserving the original behavior: each complete NUL-delimited record gets decoded as UTF8, output via Write-Output, and then the waiter signal fires immediately after. Since the waiter rescans the entire output collection on each signal, signaling once per record maintains the expected responsiveness, even if multiple complete records arrive in a single Read() chunk.

Writing the replacement script... Refining the logic to match the original exactly: on each record boundary (when a 0 byte is encountered), I emit output only if there are pending bytes, then always signal regardless. At EOF, I handle any remaining buffered data the same way before returning.

For the buffer size, 4096 bytes works fine—even though records can span megabytes (like large environment payloads), the pending list just accumulates across multiple Read() calls until hitting the NUL terminator.

I'll go with 8192 bytes as a better middle ground; it amortizes syscall overhead without being excessive. The key win here is that I'm now batching the byte-by-byte parsing into chunks from the stream rather than making individual native calls for each byte, which eliminates the expensive per-iteration overhead.

Actually, I could skip the per-byte loop entirely and use `IndexOf` to find NUL boundaries directly in the buffered data, which would be even faster than iterating through the array manually.

Writing streaming reader script... Writing buffer parsing logic... The optimization delivered a massive speedup—dropping from 16.79ms to 0.36ms per cycle for that 1.8KB payload read—which confirms we've hit the exact bottleneck. This should comfortably bring the total cycle time back under the 33ms threshold we need. Moving on to Task B now.

For the filler/parentBackground regression, the core issue is that `shouldFill` gets called after `renderBlockSegments` has already cleared the color state via `terminal.String()`, so when the filler template tries to reference `<parentBackground>`, that context is gone. I need to either capture the tail segment's colors into engine state before the reset happens and restore them before rendering the filler, or shift when the reset occurs so it doesn't wipe the state prematurely.

Looking at the actual usage, `shouldFill` is always padding within the same block (like right-aligning content within that block), not between blocks, so semantically the filler should still see the colors from the segments that were just rendered in that same block.

The cleanest fix is to capture the last active segment's colors before they get cleared, then pass that captured context directly to the filler rendering instead of relying on global stack timing. I'll extract the `ParentColors` reset into its own explicit function and call it precisely when starting a new block's segment loop in the prompt package, rather than in the terminal's String method. I see that `previousActiveSegment` is actually cleared at the END of each block's render, not the start, which means it persists through all segments within a single block and only gets nil'd once that block is completely finished. Since `shouldFill` runs right after `renderBlockSegments` returns but before `previousActiveSegment` is cleared, the timing works out so that the filler logic for block N sees the segment state from that same block's last segment, not from a previous block.

The real issue is that `ParentColors` shouldn't be reset inside `terminal.String()` at all — instead, I should reset it explicitly at the START of each new block's rendering, right before processing its first segment, which mirrors how `previousActiveSegment` is managed but at the natural boundary where each block begins. I need to figure out where to place the reset call in the iteration loop—ideally once per block before rendering, rather than duplicating it across multiple functions. But there's a deeper question: should each block start with a fresh parent color chain, or should colors carry over between blocks to allow gradient effects to bleed across them? The original code's pattern of clearing `previousActiveSegment` at the end of each block suggests blocks were meant to be isolated, but multi-block prompts might benefit from continuity.

Looking at where `SetParentColors` gets called alongside `previousActiveSegment` updates in the rendering logic, the intended design appears to be per-block scoping—each block resets its parent color context rather than inheriting from the previous block's final state.

The problem is that the current reset happens too frequently: every time `String()` is called, including when `shouldFill` internally invokes it to flush filler text. This causes a premature reset before the filler itself can use the parent colors that were set. The fix needs to target block boundaries specifically—resetting once per actual block transition, not once per `String()` invocation. So the real issue is that `renderBlockSegments` already clears ParentColors with its own `String()` call before `shouldFill` even executes, meaning the filler logic can't access the last segment's parent color no matter what. Simply deferring the reset to block boundaries won't help here since the segments have already been rendered and their colors lost by the time the filler needs them. To fix this, I'd need to preserve the parent color state across that boundary somehow.

The cleaner approach is to capture the tail parent color explicitly before the reset happens, then have `shouldFill` restore that captured value into the terminal's ParentColors right before rendering the filler — that way the timing of the global reset doesn't matter, since we're deliberately seeding back what's needed at the exact moment it's needed. I should look at where `renderBlockSegments` is called and how to wire this capture-and-restore pattern in. Implementing the fix... Adding the field to Engine and capturing tail colors... The reset clears ParentColors back to an empty slice after the filler's String() call, which is fine since the blockText's Write already consumed that seeded entry during renderBlockSegments. The same fix applies to the other writeBlock path at line 268 when Overflow is Hide, since it uses the same shouldFill mechanism. I should also check whether the transient prompt path in extra.go has a similar filler mechanism that needs the same seeding fix.

1	package prompt
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"strings"
6	
7		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/color"
8		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/config"
9		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/shell"
10		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/template"
11		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/terminal"
12	)
13	
14	type ExtraPromptType int
15	
16	const (
17		Transient ExtraPromptType = iota
18		Valid
19		Error
20		Secondary
21		Debug
22	)
23	
24	func (e *Engine) ExtraPrompt(promptType ExtraPromptType) string {
25		var prompt *config.Segment
26	
27		switch promptType {
28		case Debug:
29			prompt = e.Config.DebugPrompt
30		case Transient:
31			prompt = e.Config.TransientPrompt
32		case Valid:
33			prompt = e.Config.ValidLine
34		case Error:
35			prompt = e.Config.ErrorLine
36		case Secondary:
37			prompt = e.Config.SecondaryPrompt
38		}
39	
40		if prompt == nil {
41			prompt = &config.Segment{}
42		}
43	
44		getTemplate := func(template string) string {
45			if len(template) != 0 {
46				return template
47			}
48			switch promptType {
49			case Debug:
50				return "[DBG]: "
51			case Transient:
52				return "{{ .Shell }}> "
53			case Secondary:
54				return "> "
55			default:
56				return ""
57			}
58		}
59	
60		promptText, err := template.RenderTrusted(getTemplate(prompt.Template), nil)
61		if err != nil {
62			promptText = err.Error()
63		}
64	
65		if promptType == Transient && prompt.Newline && !e.cancelNewline() {
66			promptText = fmt.Sprintf("%s%s", e.getNewline(), promptText)
67		}
68	
69		if promptType == Transient && e.Config.ShellIntegration {
70			exitCode, _ := e.Env.StatusCodes()
71			e.write(terminal.CommandFinished(exitCode, e.Env.Flags().NoExitCode))
72			e.write(terminal.PromptStart())
73		}
74	
75		foreground := color.Ansi(prompt.ForegroundTemplates.FirstMatch(nil, string(prompt.Foreground)))
76		background := color.Ansi(prompt.BackgroundTemplates.FirstMatch(nil, string(prompt.Background)))
77		terminal.SetColors(background, foreground)
78		terminal.Write(background, foreground, promptText)
79	
80		str, length := terminal.String()
81	
82		if promptType == Secondary && e.Env.Shell() == shell.ZSH && e.Env.Flags().Eval {
83			evalOutput := fmt.Sprintf("_omp_secondary_prompt=%s", shell.QuotePosixStr(str))
84			evalOutput += fmt.Sprintf("\nPOSH_MULTILINE_KEEPPROMPT=%t", prompt.MultilineKeepPrompt)
85			return evalOutput
86		}
87	
88		if promptType != Transient {
89			return str
90		}
91	
92		rightStr, rightLength := e.renderRightTemplate(prompt, background, foreground)
93	
94		var padText string
95		if len(prompt.Filler) != 0 {
96			consoleWidth, err := e.Env.TerminalWidth()
97			if err == nil || consoleWidth != 0 {
98				padText, _ = e.shouldFill(prompt.Filler, consoleWidth-length-rightLength)
99			}
100		}
101	
102		// for pwsh, the padding moves inside the cursor save/restore sequence
103		// when a right-aligned template is rendered, see transientPWSH
104		if e.Env.Shell() != shell.PWSH || rightLength == 0 {
105			str += padText
106		}
107	
108		switch e.Env.Shell() {
109		case shell.ZSH:
110			if !e.Env.Flags().Eval {
111				return str
112			}
113	
114			return e.transientZSH(str, rightStr)
115		case shell.PWSH:
116			return e.transientPWSH(str, padText, rightStr, length, rightLength)
117		}
118	
119		return str
120	}
121	
122	// transientZSH returns the transient prompt as an eval statement setting
123	// both PS1 and RPROMPT, letting zsh align the right-aligned template natively.
124	func (e *Engine) transientZSH(str, rightStr string) string {
125		// Warp doesn't support RPROMPT
126		if e.isWarp() {
127			rightStr = ""
128		}
129	
130		prompt := fmt.Sprintf("PS1=%s", shell.QuotePosixStr(str))
131		prompt += fmt.Sprintf("\nRPROMPT=%s", shell.QuotePosixStr(rightStr))
132		return prompt
133	}
134	
135	// transientPWSH appends the right-aligned template to the transient prompt by
136	// writing the gap and the right-aligned text, then restoring the cursor to
137	// right after the left part so the accepted command is drawn there,
138	// mirroring writePrimaryRightPrompt.
139	func (e *Engine) transientPWSH(str, padText, rightStr string, length, rightLength int) string {
140		// clear the line afterwards to prevent text from being written on the same line
141		// see https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/3628
142		str += terminal.ClearAfter()
143	
144		if rightLength == 0 {
145			return str
146		}
147	
148		consoleWidth, err := e.Env.TerminalWidth()
149		if err != nil || consoleWidth == 0 {
150			return str
151		}
152	
153		gap := consoleWidth - length - rightLength
154		if gap < 0 {
155			return str
156		}
157	
158		if len(padText) == 0 {
159			padText = strings.Repeat(" ", gap)
160		}
161	
162		return str + terminal.SaveCursorPosition() + padText + rightStr + terminal.RestoreCursorPosition()
163	}
164	
165	// TransientRPrompt renders only the transient prompt's right-aligned template.
166	// Shells with a native right prompt function use this independently from the
167	// transient prompt's left side.
168	func (e *Engine) TransientRPrompt() string {
169		prompt := e.Config.TransientPrompt
170		if prompt == nil {
171			return ""
172		}
173	
174		foreground := color.Ansi(prompt.ForegroundTemplates.FirstMatch(nil, string(prompt.Foreground)))
175		background := color.Ansi(prompt.BackgroundTemplates.FirstMatch(nil, string(prompt.Background)))
176		terminal.SetColors(background, foreground)
177	
178		str, _ := e.renderRightTemplate(prompt, background, foreground)
179		return str
180	}
181	
182	// renderRightTemplate renders the transient prompt's right-aligned template.
183	// Only shells with a supported native or emulated right prompt can display it.
184	func (e *Engine) renderRightTemplate(prompt *config.Segment, background, foreground color.Ansi) (string, int) {
185		if len(prompt.RightTemplate) == 0 {
186			return "", 0
187		}
188	
189		switch e.Env.Shell() {
190		case shell.ZSH, shell.PWSH, shell.FISH:
191		default:
192			return "", 0
193		}
194	
195		text, err := template.RenderTrusted(prompt.RightTemplate, nil)
196		if err != nil {
197			text = err.Error()
198		}
199	
200		terminal.Write(background, foreground, text)
201		return terminal.String()
202	}

60	func (e *Engine) write(txt string) {
61		// Grow capacity proactively if needed
62		if e.prompt.Cap() < e.prompt.Len()+len(txt) {
63			e.prompt.Grow(len(txt) * 2) // Grow by double the needed size to reduce future allocations
64		}
65		e.prompt.WriteString(txt)
66	}
67	
68	func (e *Engine) string() string {
69		txt := e.prompt.String()
70		e.prompt.Reset()
71		return txt
72	}
73	
74	func (e *Engine) canWriteRightBlock(length int, rprompt bool) (int, bool) {
75		if rprompt && (e.rprompt == "") {
76			return 0, false
77		}
78	
79		consoleWidth, err := e.Env.TerminalWidth()
80		if err != nil || consoleWidth == 0 {
81			return 0, false
82		}
83	
84		availableSpace := consoleWidth - e.currentLineLength
85	
86		// spanning multiple lines
87		if availableSpace < 0 {
88			overflow := e.currentLineLength % consoleWidth
89			availableSpace = consoleWidth - overflow
90		}
91	
92		availableSpace -= length
93	
94		promptBreathingRoom := 5
95		if rprompt {
96			promptBreathingRoom = 30
97		}
98	
99		canWrite := availableSpace >= promptBreathingRoom
100	
101		// reset the available space when we can't write so we can fill the line
102		if !canWrite {
103			availableSpace = consoleWidth - length
104		}
105	
106		return availableSpace, canWrite
107	}
108	
109	func (e *Engine) pwd() {
110		// only print when relevant
111		if e.Config.PWD == "" {
112			return
113		}
114	
115		// only print when supported
116		sh := e.Env.Shell()
117		if sh == shell.ELVISH || sh == shell.XONSH {
118			return
119		}
120	
121		pwd := e.Env.Pwd()
122		if e.Env.IsCygwin() {
123			pwd = strings.ReplaceAll(pwd, `\`, `/`)
124		}
125	
126		// Allow template logic to define when to enable the PWD (when supported)
127		pwdType, err := template.RenderTrusted(e.Config.PWD, nil)
128		if err != nil || pwdType == "" {
129			return
130		}
131	
132		// Convert to Windows path when in WSL
133		if e.Env.IsWsl() {
134			pwd = e.Env.ConvertToWindowsPath(pwd)
135		}
136	
137		user := e.Env.User()
138		host, _ := e.Env.Host()
139		e.write(terminal.Pwd(pwdType, user, host, pwd))
140	}
141	
142	func (e *Engine) getNewline() string {
143		newline := "\n"
144	
145		if e.Plain || e.Env.Flags().Debug {
146			return newline
147		}
148	
149		// Warp terminal will remove a newline character ('\n') from the prompt, so we hack it in.
150		if e.isWarp() {
151			return terminal.LineBreak()
152		}
153	
154		return newline
155	}
156	
157	func (e *Engine) writeNewline() {
158		defer func() {
159			e.currentLineLength = 0
160		}()
161	
162		e.write(e.getNewline())
163	}
164	
165	func (e *Engine) isWarp() bool {
166		return terminal.Program == terminal.Warp
167	}
168	
169	func (e *Engine) isIterm() bool {
170		return terminal.Program == terminal.ITerm
171	}
172	
173	func (e *Engine) shouldFill(filler string, padLength int) (string, bool) {
174		if filler == "" {
175			log.Debug("no filler specified")
176			return "", false
177		}
178	
179		if padLength < 0 {
180			log.Debug("padding length is negative")
181			return "", false
182		}
183	
184		e.Padding = padLength
185	
186		defer func() {
187			e.Padding = 0
188		}()
189	
190		var err error
191		if filler, err = template.RenderTrusted(filler, e); err != nil {
192			return "", false
193		}
194	
195		// allow for easy color overrides and templates
196		terminal.SetColors("default", "default")
197	
198		// the block's own segments already reset the parent stack when their
199		// terminal.String() call produced blockText - reseed the one entry a
200		// <parentBackground>/<parentForeground> anchor in the filler needs.
201		if e.blockTailColors != nil {
202			terminal.ParentColors = append(terminal.ParentColors, e.blockTailColors)
203		}
204	
205		terminal.Write("", "", filler)
206		filler, lenFiller := terminal.String()
207		if lenFiller == 0 {
208			log.Debug("filler has no length")
209			return "", false
210		}
211	
212		repeat := padLength / lenFiller
213		unfilled := padLength % lenFiller
214		txt := strings.Repeat(filler, repeat) + strings.Repeat(" ", unfilled)
215		log.Debug("filling with", txt)
216		return txt, true
217	}

content.thinking:
The test is passing with the tail colors being resolved correctly, but I'm noticing something odd about how the color is being applied — it's showing up as a foreground escape code rather than a background code, which is unexpected given the `parentBackground` anchor syntax I was testing for.

Looking at how the anchor gets parsed, `<parentBackground>` is being interpreted as a single-color anchor without a comma, so it matches the foreground group in the regex pattern and sets the foreground channel instead of the background. The keyword "parentBackground" itself is being resolved as a foreground color value through the color resolution system, which explains why I'm seeing the ANSI foreground code in the output rather than a background code. The baseline test without the tail correctly shows no blue color, just the reverse video styling, confirming that when `ParentColors` is nil, it properly resolves to Transparent rather than fabricating a color. This matches the expected behavior for the nil-parents path, distinct from the pre-existing edge case Fable noted where an empty-but-non-nil slice incorrectly returns the raw keyword string instead of Transparent.

That inconsistency is harmless since unresolvable keywords degrade to no color anyway in the ANSI conversion, so it's not worth fixing as part of this task. The fix is working as intended, so I'll clean up the temporary test file.

input.command:
cd D:/oh-my-posh && git add src/shell/scripts/omp.ps1 && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
perf(shell): read pwsh streaming records in chunks, not bytes

The serve daemon reader ($script:StreamingReaderScript) drained stdout
one $stream.ReadByte() call per byte. Fine at the sub-KB record sizes
streaming shipped with, but a gradient-heavy prompt record runs
1-2KB, and a PowerShell method-call per byte to drain it measured at
~9.3us/byte - ~17ms for one cycle's records on this machine, ~47x
slower than a chunked read of the same payload.

PowerShell's prompt function blocks synchronously on one full
render round trip per Enter, and buffered keystrokes each need their
own round trip - so drain time was always queued-Enters x per-Enter
cost. What changed is which side of the ~33ms key-repeat interval
that cost sits on: under it, the queue never grows and releasing
Enter stops the prompt instantly; over it, holding Enter grows an
unbounded backlog that then drains visibly for seconds after release.
Gradients pushed a real theme over that line.

Read in 4KB chunks and split on NUL via [Array]::IndexOf instead of a
per-byte loop. Verified byte-identical output against the old
implementation across chunk-boundary, empty-record, unicode, and
unterminated-trailing-record cases at buffer sizes from 1 to 4096.
EOF
)"

cd D:/oh-my-posh && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(prompt): resolve parentBackground in a block's own filler

cdb4bfe8 scoped terminal.ParentColors to one block, resetting it in
String() once a block's segments are done rendering. block.Filler (and
the transient prompt's filler) render after that reset - via
shouldFill, called from writeBlock with an already-built blockText -
so a filler template using <parentBackground>/<parentForeground>
started resolving against an empty stack instead of the block's last
segment.

Capture the last segment's colors into a new Engine.blockTailColors
field right before previousActiveSegment resets to nil (same spot in
both renderBlockFromCache and renderBlockSegments), and reseed
terminal.ParentColors with that one entry in shouldFill before writing
the filler. No stock theme uses filler with parent color keywords, so
this was unreachable in practice, but it's a real behavior change from
what shipped in cdb4bfe8.
EOF
)"

content.text:
Both fixed and committed, tree clean.

- [3096d26b](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/commit/3096d26b) — `omp.ps1` reader: chunked reads + `[Array]::IndexOf` instead of per-byte `ReadByte()`. Verified byte-identical output across chunk-boundary/empty/unicode/unterminated cases at buffer sizes 1-4096. Measured 16.79ms → 0.36ms per cycle on a gradient-theme-sized record — puts you back well under the ~33ms key-repeat line, Enter spam should stop instantly again.
- [76ac76d6](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/commit/76ac76d6) — filler `parentBackground`/`parentForeground` regression from our earlier fix. Verified via a scratch test: blue tail color now correctly reaches the filler anchor; nil case correctly stays unresolved.

Gradient cost itself: confirmed negligible (µs-level), not touched — wasn't the real problem.

Build/vet/test clean across the whole module (3 pre-existing unsafe.Pointer vet notes in `cache/file_map_windows.go`, unrelated to any of this).

Restart your pwsh session (or `oh-my-posh disable notice` isn't needed — just a fresh shell) to pick up the new init script.
